sections.label: normal
sections.text: You've likely heard the story, right? It's about a guy that's teaching two classes.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: And one class, they're doing pottery, but it's a game of volume, quantity.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: So every week they are throwing pots and vases and all that stuff.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And he's teaching them about it, but they are producing quantity and quantity and quantity

sections.label: normal
sections.text: of these pots and vases and everything else.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: The other class, they're going to have one final.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: They're going to throw one pot, one vase.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And that's going to be at the end of the semester.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: But the whole semester they're learning about structure and firmness and shape and physics

sections.label: normal
sections.text: and technique and they're learning all that.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And here comes the final and he gets all the students to give him their best work

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: and then he has to choose, right? And the story goes, right?

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: That all the top products that came out of it were from the

sections.label: critical
sections.text: quantity, not quality side. And you start scratching your head.

sections.label: critical
sections.text: You're like, what's going on? And what you learn, right?

sections.label: normal
sections.text: From the story, whether it was pure research or whether it was pure fabrication, I don't know.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: I read it in the book, but what the moral of the story is that

sections.label: critical
sections.text: it's more important to learn by making something, by doing something

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: than it is to learn about the making of something or the doing of something.

sections.label: critical
sections.text: Like you, you got to actually get the reps.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: You got to do it over and over and that's how you get better at what you're doing.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And so when technology costs nothing anymore, right?

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: When you can produce code, 20,000 lines of code

sections.label: critical
sections.text: in an hour for free, then what do you do next?

sections.label: critical
sections.text: How do you maneuver when it's free, right?

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And I go back to that story of the notion of how they made the clay pots.

sections.label: critical
sections.text: And I just think the name of the game is quantity. It's always been quantity. It's always been reps.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: And what tends to happen when you're using AI agents to build your software

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: is you tend to generate something and then you iterate on making it better and better and better.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: And so you're basically voting on one edition, one version of your product.

sections.label: normal
sections.text: You might've made or the agents might've made an architectural decision early on

sections.label: normal
sections.text: that wasn't a good one. Well, you're just going to keep iterating on trying to make that a little bit better until you're done.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: Whereas what I did the other day was I generated four versions of the same application.

sections.label: critical
sections.text: I literally sent agents to go build version A, B, C, and D.

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: And then I had another team review all the code for all four versions. And when it evaluated the best features, the best components, the best decisions

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: made across all four code bases,

sections.label: critical
sections.text: it generated a fifth version.

sections.label: critical
sections.text: And that fifth version was the best of all, right?

sections.label: emphasis
sections.text: Take from everything that you've seen and everything you've learned and assemble a fifth version

sections.label: critical
sections.text: and it came out and it was brilliant

sections.label: critical
sections.text: because more reps is better

sections.label: critical
sections.text: because analyzing what's actually done and saying, I like that approach or that decision. Why wouldn't you generate it four times, instead of just once.
